mission.title: Install and Configure KubeAI
mission.description: KubeAI is an AI Inference Operator for Kubernetes designed to serve machine learning models, including LLMs and embeddings, in production environments. It simplifies the deployment and scaling of AI models while optimizing performance through intelligent load balancing. Key trade-offs include the need for adequate resource allocation and potential costs associated with scaling.

steps.title: Install the Helm CLI
steps.description:
This step installs the Helm CLI, which is required to manage Kubernetes applications.

```bash

curl https://raw.githubusercontent.com/helm/helm/main/scripts/get-helm-3 | bash

```

> This installs the latest version of Helm 3.

steps.title: Add the KubeAI Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the KubeAI Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add kubeai https://charts.kubeai.org

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the KubeAI repository.
> This updates your local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Create a Kubernetes cluster
steps.description:
This command creates a new Kubernetes cluster with the specified version and node count.

```bash

gcloud container clusters create kubeai-cluster --num-nodes=3 --region=us-central1 --cluster-version=1.31

```

> Adjust the region and node count as needed.

steps.title: Set up kubeconfig
steps.description:
This command configures your local kubeconfig to connect to the newly created cluster.

```bash

gcloud container clusters get-credentials kubeai-cluster --region=us-central1

```

> This command retrieves the cluster credentials.

steps.title: Install KubeAI
steps.description:
This command installs the KubeAI Helm chart in the kube-system namespace.

```bash

helm install kubeai kubeai/kubeai --namespace kube-system --version 0.23.1

```

> This installs KubeAI with the specified version.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the KubeAI pods to ensure they are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> Look for the kubeai pods and ensure they are in the Running state.

uninstall.title: Uninstall KubeAI
uninstall.description:
This step removes the KubeAI installation and warns about potential data loss.

```bash

helm uninstall kubeai --namespace kube-system

kubectl delete namespace kube-system

```

> This command removes KubeAI from your cluster.
> This will delete the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade KubeAI
upgrade.description:
This step includes backing up existing configurations, upgrading KubeAI, and verifying the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl get all -n kube-system -o yaml > kubeai-backup.yaml

helm upgrade kubeai kubeai/kubeai --namespace kube-system --version 0.23.1

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> Backup current KubeAI configurations.
> Upgrade KubeAI to the latest version.
> Verify that the KubeAI pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get all -n kube-system -o yaml > kubeai-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Error: unable to connect to the server: dial tcp <IP>:<PORT>: connect: connection refused'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Kubernetes API server is not reachable, possibly due to network issues or the cluster not being up.

**Fix:**
Check the cluster status with 'gcloud container clusters list' and ensure it's running.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Error: release kubeai failed: timed out waiting for the condition'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This suggests that the KubeAI installation is taking too long, possibly due to insufficient resources.

**Fix:**
Ensure your cluster has enough resources (minimum 2 vCPU, 4GB RAM per node) and retry the installation.

troubleshooting.title: Pods in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the KubeAI pods are failing to start, likely due to misconfiguration or resource limits.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kube-system' for more details.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'no matches for kind "KubeAI" in version "v1alpha1"'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) for KubeAI are not installed.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the CRDs are installed by running 'kubectl get crd' and verify KubeAI CRDs are present.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new model types and improved load balancing algorithms.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from a previous version, ensure to backup your configurations as noted in the upgrade steps.

resolution.summary: You should see the KubeAI pods running with 'kubectl get pods -n kube-system' showing all pods in the Running state.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and the necessary tools installed.
